enrichment.short_description: OneDev is a free, open-source, self-hosted DevOps platform that unifies Git repository hosting, CI/CD pipelines, kanban-style issue tracking, code search, and package registries into a single Java application.
enrichment.seo_title: OneDev Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OneDev review 2026: an all-in-one self-hosted Git server with built-in CI/CD, kanban issue tracking, and package registries. Compare features, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OneDev is an open-source, self-hosted DevOps platform created by developer Robin Shen that unifies Git repository management, a Kubernetes-native CI/CD engine, kanban-style issue tracking, code search and navigation, and built-in package registries (Docker, NPM, Maven, NuGet, PyPI, RubyGems, Helm) into a single Java application distributed as one Docker image. Its core differentiator versus GitLab or Gitea is depth of integration: pull requests, CI/CD jobs, and issues share the same data model, so reviewers can jump between commits, builds, and tickets without leaving the app, and CI/CD jobs can be authored through a visual GUI instead of hand-written YAML. OneDev also includes per-commit code search by symbol, file, or regular expression; inline commenting on any code snippet without opening a pull request; customizable issue states and fields; an email-based service desk; LDAP/Active Directory authentication with SSO and two-factor auth; and more recently AI-assisted user task assignment. The project is hosted on GitHub at theonedev/onedev with over 15,000 stars and nearly 500 releases, with active development continuing through 2026 (latest release v16.2.0). OneDev ships two editions: the Community Edition is completely free and open source, covering the full DevOps toolchain described above and suitable for individual developers, small teams, and self-hosters who want a GitLab alternative without licensing costs. The Enterprise Edition costs $6 per user per month (12 user-month minimum) and adds high-availability clustering without shared storage, cross-project code search, built-in security and secret scanning, Renovate dependency-update integration, audit logging, time tracking and timesheets, a web terminal for debugging CI/CD jobs, SendGrid and Microsoft Teams integrations, and priority email support with a two-business-day response guarantee. OneDev targets engineering teams, DevOps engineers, and self-hosting enthusiasts who want to consolidate Git hosting, CI/CD, and issue tracking into one lightweight tool rather than stitching together GitHub, Jenkins, and Jira separately. Its primary competitors are GitLab (self-managed and SaaS), Gitea and its fork Forgejo, SourceHut, and Codeberg, though OneDev distinguishes itself with a built-in visual CI/CD job editor and tighter cross-feature integration for a single maintainer-led, low-overhead product. It deploys via Docker, bare-metal installers, or Kubernetes and is written entirely in Java.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OneDev is a self-hosted, open-source DevOps platform that combines a Git server, CI/CD engine, issue tracker with kanban boards, code search, and package registries into one unified application. It was built to reduce the sprawl that comes from wiring together separate tools like GitHub, Jenkins, and Jira.

Because every feature shares the same underlying data model, teams can navigate seamlessly between commits, pull requests, CI/CD build logs, and issues. The project is written in Java, ships as a single Docker image, and is actively maintained with frequent releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OneDev's CI/CD engine is Kubernetes-native and Docker-first, with a visual job builder that lets teams define pipelines without hand-writing YAML, plus support for matrix jobs, typed parameters, and reusable job logic.

Built-in package registries cover Docker, NPM, Maven, NuGet, PyPI, RubyGems, and Helm, so teams do not need a separate artifact repository. Code search works per-commit by symbol, file name, or regular expression, and pull requests support inline commenting on any code snippet, not just diff lines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Community Edition is free and open source, and includes the full DevOps toolchain: Git hosting, CI/CD, issue tracking, code search, and package registries, plus LDAP/Active Directory authentication and single sign-on.

The Enterprise Edition costs $6 per user per month with a 12 user-month minimum purchase, and layers on high availability, security and secret scanning, audit logging, time tracking, and priority support for organizations with compliance or scale requirements.

features.title: Integrated Git hosting
features.description: Full Git repository management with per-commit, symbol-aware code search and navigation.

features.title: Visual CI/CD engine
features.description: Kubernetes-native, Docker-first build pipelines configurable through a GUI instead of YAML, with matrix jobs and reusable job logic.

features.title: Kanban issue tracking
features.description: Customizable issue states, fields, and links, with an email-based service desk for external issue creation.

features.title: Built-in package registries
features.description: Native support for Docker, NPM, Maven, NuGet, PyPI, RubyGems, and Helm packages without a separate artifact server.

features.title: Inline code commenting
features.description: Comment on any code snippet directly, independent of pull requests, for lightweight code review.

features.title: Enterprise security scanning
features.description: Vulnerability and secret scanning available in the Enterprise Edition for compliance-focused teams.

features.title: SSO and directory integration
features.description: LDAP, Active Directory, single sign-on, and two-factor authentication available in the free Community Edition.

pros: Community Edition is fully free and open source with no artificial feature caps on the core toolchain
Combines Git hosting, CI/CD, issue tracking, and package registries in one lightweight self-hosted app
Visual CI/CD job builder lowers the barrier to writing pipelines compared to YAML-only tools
Active development with frequent releases and a sizeable open-source community
Simple single-Docker-image deployment keeps self-hosting overhead low
cons: Smaller ecosystem and community than GitLab or GitHub, meaning fewer third-party integrations
Enterprise features like HA clustering and security scanning require a paid per-user license
Maintained primarily by a small core team rather than a large corporate backer
Documentation and learning resources are less extensive than more established DevOps platforms
Java-based runtime can be heavier on resources than lighter Git servers like Gitea

faqs.question: Is OneDev really free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The OneDev Community Edition is free and open source under its published license, and includes Git hosting, CI/CD, issue tracking, code search, and package registries. Only the Enterprise Edition, which adds features like high availability and security scanning, requires a paid subscription.

faqs.question: What is included in OneDev Enterprise Edition?
faqs.answer: Enterprise Edition costs $6 per user per month with a 12 user-month minimum, and adds high-availability clustering, cross-project code search, vulnerability and secret scanning, audit logging, time tracking, a web terminal for CI/CD debugging, and priority support.

faqs.question: How is OneDev different from GitLab?
faqs.answer: OneDev is a lighter-weight, single-binary self-hosted alternative that bundles Git hosting, CI/CD, and issue tracking similarly to GitLab, but with a smaller footprint, a visual CI/CD job builder, and a smaller (though active) open-source community compared to GitLab's much larger ecosystem.

faqs.question: Can OneDev run CI/CD jobs without writing YAML?
faqs.answer: Yes. OneDev provides a GUI for building CI/CD jobs with typed parameters, matrix jobs, and reusable logic, though YAML-based pipeline definitions are also supported for teams that prefer them.

faqs.question: Does OneDev support Docker and package registries?
faqs.answer: Yes. OneDev includes built-in registries for Docker, NPM, Maven, NuGet, PyPI, RubyGems, and Helm packages, eliminating the need for a separate artifact repository like Nexus or Artifactory.

faqs.question: How do I deploy OneDev?
faqs.answer: OneDev ships as a single Docker image and can also be installed as a standalone application or deployed on Kubernetes, making self-hosting straightforward for teams of any size.

faqs.question: Who maintains OneDev?
faqs.answer: OneDev was created by developer Robin Shen and is maintained as an active open-source project on GitHub, with nearly 500 releases and over 15,000 stars as of 2026.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted Git and CI/CD for small engineering teams
use_cases.description: Small teams replace separate GitHub, Jenkins, and Jira subscriptions with one free, self-hosted OneDev instance covering source control, builds, and issue tracking.

use_cases.title: Compliance-driven internal DevOps platform
use_cases.description: Organizations with data residency or compliance requirements self-host OneDev Enterprise for full control over source code, build infrastructure, and audit trails.

use_cases.title: Package and artifact consolidation
use_cases.description: Teams use OneDev's built-in Docker, NPM, and Maven registries to avoid running a separate package manager server alongside their Git host.

features: Package registries (Docker, NPM, Maven, NuGet, PyPI, RubyGems, Helm)

enrichment.short_description: Oneflow is a Swedish contract lifecycle management platform that turns static PDF contracts into dynamic, data-rich digital documents with e-signing, templates, and workflow automation.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (basic PDF signing); paid plans from EUR17/user/month
enrichment.seo_title: Oneflow Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Oneflow review 2026: contract lifecycle management with e-signing, templates and automation. See pricing plans, features, pros, cons and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Oneflow is a contract lifecycle management (CLM) platform founded in 2012 in Stockholm, Sweden by Anders Hamnes (CEO), Kalle Moodh, and Fredrik Rinman. Its core differentiator is treating contracts as structured, editable data rather than static PDF or Word documents: parties can negotiate, comment, and edit contract content live inside a shared digital document, with every field trackable as data that can feed dashboards, CRM systems like Salesforce and HubSpot, and reporting tools. Oneflow covers the full contract lifecycle, including template creation, collaborative drafting, legally binding e-signatures (including advanced and qualified signature levels for EU compliance), approval workflows, contract analytics, and post-signature lifecycle management such as renewal reminders and obligation tracking. The company went public in April 2022, listing its shares on Nasdaq First North Premier Growth Market in Stockholm, and as of 2026 employs roughly 127-159 people across offices in Sweden, Norway, and Finland, with customers across Europe and beyond. Oneflow's pricing is tiered: a Free plan allows sending and signing basic PDF contracts at no cost; the Essentials plan (around EUR17 per user per month) adds digital, data-driven contracts and templates; the Business plan (around EUR45 per user per month) adds contract lifecycle management, advanced workflow automation, and premium integrations; and an Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with dedicated support, advanced security, and API access for large organizations. Oneflow targets small and mid-sized businesses through enterprise sales, legal, HR, and procurement teams that need to move contract workflows away from manual PDF-and-email processes toward an auditable, integrated system. Its primary competitors include DocuSign (particularly DocuSign CLM), PandaDoc, Ironclad, Concord, and SignNow, with Oneflow differentiating itself through its 'living document' data model, native CRM integrations, and strong presence in the Nordic and broader European markets. As a publicly traded SaaS company, Oneflow publishes regular financial disclosures, which lends transparency around its growth trajectory that many private CLM competitors do not offer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Oneflow is a contract lifecycle management platform built around the idea that contracts should be structured, editable data rather than static documents. Founded in Stockholm in 2012, the company has grown into a publicly traded SaaS business listed on Nasdaq First North Premier Growth Market since 2022.

The platform lets sales, legal, HR, and procurement teams draft, negotiate, e-sign, and manage contracts in one place, with every contract field trackable for reporting and integration with CRM and ERP tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Oneflow's signature feature is its live, collaborative contract editor, where both parties can edit and comment on contract terms in real time before signing, rather than exchanging redlined PDF versions.

The platform also includes customizable templates, legally binding e-signatures with multiple compliance levels, approval workflows, contract analytics dashboards, and native integrations with tools like Salesforce, HubSpot, and Microsoft Dynamics.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Oneflow offers a free tier for basic PDF sending and signing, making it accessible for very small teams or occasional use.

Paid plans scale from Essentials (around EUR17/user/month) for digital contracts and templates, to Business (around EUR45/user/month) for full lifecycle management and premium integrations, up to custom Enterprise pricing for large organizations.

features.title: Live collaborative contract editing
features.description: Both parties can edit and comment on contract content in a shared digital document before signing, instead of exchanging static PDF redlines.

features.title: Data-driven contracts
features.description: Every contract field is captured as structured data, feeding dashboards, reporting, and downstream systems instead of being locked inside a PDF.

features.title: E-signatures with compliance levels
features.description: Supports standard, advanced, and qualified e-signature levels to meet EU and other regional legal requirements.

features.title: Template management
features.description: Reusable, branded contract templates speed up creation for sales, HR, and procurement teams.

features.title: Workflow automation and approvals
features.description: Configurable approval chains and automated reminders reduce manual follow-up on contract status.

features.title: CRM and business tool integrations
features.description: Native integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot, and Microsoft Dynamics keep contract data in sync with the rest of the sales stack.

features.title: Contract analytics
features.description: Dashboards surface contract value, cycle time, and renewal data for legal and revenue operations teams.

pros: Live, collaborative editing feels more natural than redlining static PDF documents
Free tier makes basic e-signing accessible without a paid commitment
Publicly traded company provides financial transparency uncommon among CLM competitors
Strong native integrations with popular CRM platforms like Salesforce and HubSpot
Contract data is structured and reportable rather than locked in unstructured documents
cons: Advanced contract lifecycle management features require the higher-priced Business or Enterprise tiers
Smaller global brand recognition compared to DocuSign or Adobe Sign
Some users report a learning curve when moving from simple e-signature tools
Deeper customization and integrations may require Enterprise-tier custom pricing
Primarily strongest in European markets, with less mature localization elsewhere

faqs.question: Is Oneflow free to use?
faqs.answer: Oneflow offers a free plan that allows sending and signing basic PDF contracts at no cost. More advanced features like data-driven digital contracts, templates, and lifecycle management require a paid Essentials, Business, or Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: How much does Oneflow cost?
faqs.answer: Oneflow's Essentials plan starts at around EUR17 per user per month for digital contracts and templates, while the Business plan is around EUR45 per user per month and adds lifecycle management and premium integrations. Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: What makes Oneflow different from DocuSign?
faqs.answer: Oneflow treats contracts as live, editable, structured data that both parties can co-edit before signing, whereas traditional e-signature tools like DocuSign are generally built around signing static PDF or Word documents.

faqs.question: Is Oneflow a publicly traded company?
faqs.answer: Yes. Oneflow AB has been listed on Nasdaq First North Premier Growth Market in Stockholm since April 2022, making its financials publicly disclosed.

faqs.question: Does Oneflow integrate with Salesforce and HubSpot?
faqs.answer: Yes, Oneflow offers native integrations with major CRM platforms including Salesforce, HubSpot, and Microsoft Dynamics to keep contract data synchronized with sales pipelines.

faqs.question: Are Oneflow e-signatures legally binding?
faqs.answer: Yes. Oneflow supports multiple e-signature compliance levels, including advanced and qualified electronic signatures, to meet legal requirements in the EU and other regions.

faqs.question: Who founded Oneflow?
faqs.answer: Oneflow was founded in 2012 in Stockholm, Sweden by Anders Hamnes, Kalle Moodh, and Fredrik Rinman.

use_cases.title: Sales contract acceleration
use_cases.description: Sales teams use Oneflow to draft, negotiate, and close deals faster with live editing and instant e-signatures synced to their CRM.

use_cases.title: HR onboarding and employment contracts
use_cases.description: HR departments send templated employment agreements and offer letters for e-signature with automated tracking of completion status.

use_cases.title: Procurement and vendor agreement management
use_cases.description: Procurement teams manage vendor contract lifecycles, renewal reminders, and approval workflows in one auditable system.

features: Send and sign basic PDF contracts

enrichment.short_description: OneNote is Microsoft's free digital notebook app for capturing, organizing, and syncing typed notes, handwriting, audio, and web clippings across Windows, Mac, mobile, and the web.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (standalone); premium AI features via Microsoft 365 from $9.99/month
languages: and dozens of other languages via Microsoft 365 localization
enrichment.seo_title: OneNote Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OneNote review 2026: Microsoft's free digital notebook with typing, handwriting, and web clipping. Compare pricing, features and alternatives like Notion.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OneNote is Microsoft's digital note-taking application, first announced in 2002 and shipped in 2003 as a standalone product bundled with certain Office and Tablet PC configurations; it became a core part of the Microsoft Office suite starting with Office 2007 and today ships both as a free standalone app and as part of Microsoft 365. OneNote organizes content into notebooks, sections, and pages, and supports free-form input including typed text, handwritten ink (especially on touch and pen-enabled devices), audio recordings, images, and clipped web content via the OneNote Web Clipper browser extension. Notes sync automatically across Windows, macOS, iOS, Android, and the web through OneDrive, making it a cross-device capture tool for students, professionals, and teams. As of 2026, OneNote's standalone app remains free to use, including unlimited notes and 5GB of OneDrive cloud storage; Microsoft has continued to invest in the standalone free product even as it retired the older, separately coded 'OneNote for Windows 10' app in October 2025 in favor of unifying development around the main desktop OneNote application. Premium capabilities, most notably Microsoft 365 Copilot integration for AI-assisted note summarization and drafting, require a Microsoft 365 subscription, which ranges from Microsoft 365 Personal at $9.99/month up to Microsoft 365 Premium at $19.99/month, bundling OneNote with Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, and additional OneDrive storage. OneNote's target audience spans students taking lecture and research notes, professionals capturing meeting notes and project documentation, and teams using shared notebooks for collaborative knowledge bases, particularly within organizations already standardized on Microsoft 365 and Teams. Its most direct competitors are Evernote, a longtime rival note-taking app; Notion, which has captured significant mindshare among teams wanting databases and wikis alongside notes; Google Keep, a simpler free alternative tied to Google Workspace; and Apple Notes, the default option for Apple device users. OneNote's competitive advantage lies in deep integration with the rest of Microsoft 365 (Outlook meeting notes, Teams meeting integration, Office document embedding) and strong handwriting and ink support on Surface and other pen-enabled Windows devices, though it is generally viewed as less flexible than Notion for building structured databases or wikis.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OneNote is Microsoft's free-form digital notebook, first released in 2003 and now a core part of the Microsoft 365 ecosystem. It lets users capture typed notes, handwriting, audio, images, and clipped web pages, organized into notebooks, sections, and pages that sync across devices via OneDrive.

Unlike many competitors, OneNote does not require notes to be organized into a rigid page structure; users can click anywhere on a page and start typing or drawing, which has made it popular for freeform brainstorming, lecture notes, and handwritten annotation on touch devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include a free-form canvas for typed and handwritten notes, a web clipper browser extension, audio note recording with text search inside recordings, and tight integration with Outlook (meeting notes) and Microsoft Teams.

Microsoft 365 subscribers also gain access to Copilot-powered AI features for summarizing and organizing notes, plus additional cloud storage and cross-app integration with Word, Excel, and PowerPoint.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OneNote's standalone app is free, including unlimited notes and 5GB of OneDrive storage, and does not require a Microsoft 365 subscription to use.

Microsoft 365 subscriptions, ranging from $9.99/month (Personal) to $19.99/month (Premium), unlock Copilot AI features inside OneNote along with the rest of the Office app suite and expanded cloud storage.

features.title: Free-form note canvas
features.description: Click anywhere on a page to type, draw, or paste content without a fixed layout structure.

features.title: Handwriting and ink support
features.description: Native pen and touch input for handwritten notes and sketches on Surface and other pen-enabled devices.

features.title: Web clipper
features.description: Browser extension for saving web pages, articles, and images directly into a OneNote notebook.

features.title: Cross-device sync via OneDrive
features.description: Notes automatically sync across Windows, macOS, iOS, Android, and the web.

features.title: Audio recording with searchable notes
features.description: Record audio during meetings or lectures and search text notes taken alongside the recording.

features.title: Outlook and Teams integration
features.description: Attach meeting notes directly to Outlook calendar events and Microsoft Teams meetings.

features.title: Copilot AI assistance
features.description: Microsoft 365 subscribers can use Copilot to summarize, organize, and draft content from notes.

pros: Completely free standalone app with no forced subscription to get core note-taking functionality
Best-in-class handwriting and ink support for pen-enabled Windows and Surface devices
Deep integration with Outlook, Teams, and the rest of Microsoft 365
Flexible free-form canvas suits both structured and unstructured note-taking styles
Reliable cross-device sync via OneDrive
cons: Less suited to building structured databases or wikis compared to tools like Notion
AI features (Copilot) require a paid Microsoft 365 subscription
Microsoft has shifted app versions over the years (retiring OneNote for Windows 10 in 2025), which has confused some long-time users
Free-form canvas can become disorganized for users who prefer strict outlines or hierarchies
Advanced search and tagging features lag behind dedicated knowledge-management tools

faqs.question: Is OneNote still free in 2026?
faqs.answer: Yes. OneNote's standalone app remains free, including unlimited notes and 5GB of OneDrive storage. A Microsoft 365 subscription is only required for premium features like Copilot AI assistance.

faqs.question: Is Microsoft discontinuing OneNote?
faqs.answer: No. Microsoft retired the separate 'OneNote for Windows 10' app in October 2025, but the main OneNote desktop and web apps continue to receive updates and new features.

faqs.question: How much does Microsoft 365 cost for OneNote's AI features?
faqs.answer: Microsoft 365 plans that unlock Copilot features in OneNote range from $9.99/month (Personal) to $12.99/month (Family) to $19.99/month (Premium) as of 2026.

faqs.question: When was OneNote first released?
faqs.answer: OneNote was announced in late 2002 and first released in 2003, initially as a standalone product before becoming part of the core Office suite starting with Office 2007.

faqs.question: Can I use OneNote without a Microsoft account?
faqs.answer: A Microsoft account is required to sync notebooks across devices via OneDrive, though local note-taking is possible without full cloud sync in some configurations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between OneNote and Notion?
faqs.answer: OneNote focuses on free-form note capture, handwriting, and Microsoft 365 integration, while Notion emphasizes structured databases, wikis, and team collaboration workflows. OneNote is free standalone; Notion has its own separate freemium pricing.

faqs.question: Does OneNote support handwriting and drawing?
faqs.answer: Yes, OneNote has strong native support for pen and touch input, making it popular on Surface devices and other pen-enabled tablets and laptops.

use_cases.title: Student lecture and research notes
use_cases.description: Students use OneNote's free-form canvas and handwriting support to capture lecture notes, diagrams, and research clippings in organized notebooks.

use_cases.title: Meeting notes tied to Outlook and Teams
use_cases.description: Professionals attach OneNote pages directly to Outlook calendar invites and Teams meetings to keep meeting notes linked to context.

use_cases.title: Personal knowledge capture and web clipping
use_cases.description: Individuals use the OneNote Web Clipper to save articles, receipts, and reference material into searchable, synced notebooks.

enrichment.short_description: OnePageCRM is a simple, action-focused CRM built around a 'Next Action' methodology that turns every contact and deal into a concrete follow-up task for small businesses and solo founders.
enrichment.seo_title: OnePageCRM Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OnePageCRM review 2026: a simple action-focused CRM for small businesses. Compare Next Action selling features, pricing plans and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OnePageCRM is a cloud-based CRM founded in 2010 by CEO Michael FitzGerald and headquartered in Galway, Ireland, with a small team of roughly 20-25 employees. Its defining feature is the 'Next Action' system: rather than presenting a traditional pipeline dashboard, OnePageCRM forces every contact to have a single, concrete next action assigned to it, which appears in a prioritized action stream reminiscent of an email inbox. The philosophy behind this design is Getting Things Done (GTD) applied to sales, aimed at eliminating the analysis paralysis and cluttered dashboards common in larger CRMs like Salesforce. Beyond the Next Action stream, OnePageCRM includes contact and deal management, a visual sales pipeline view, email integration and templates, web forms for lead capture, workflow automation rules, basic lead scoring, customizable tags and fields, and team collaboration features like shared notes and communication history. The product deliberately avoids feature bloat, positioning itself against complex enterprise CRMs by emphasizing fast onboarding, a lightweight interface, and zero unnecessary complexity. OnePageCRM's pricing starts at $9.95 per user per month for the Professional plan (billed annually; monthly billing is somewhat higher) and $19.95 per user per month for the Business plan, which adds advanced automation and reporting; both plans include a 21-day free trial, and annual billing includes several months free compared to paying monthly. There is no permanently free tier. OnePageCRM targets small and medium-sized businesses, solo founders, freelancers, and small sales teams, particularly those in consulting, professional services, and B2B sales who have found larger CRMs like Salesforce or HubSpot too complex or expensive for their needs. Its primary competitors include Pipedrive (the closest comparable in simplicity and pipeline focus), HubSpot CRM's free tier, Insightly, Nutshell, and Close CRM. OnePageCRM differentiates itself by being explicitly built around action-taking rather than passive data storage, arguing that most CRMs fail because sales reps do not know what to do next with a lead; the Next Action queue is designed to solve that problem directly. The company has raised outside investment, including a EUR575,000 investment specifically earmarked to create jobs at its Galway headquarters, reflecting its continued growth as an Irish SaaS export.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OnePageCRM is a small-business CRM built around a single organizing principle: every contact should have one clear next action. Founded in 2010 in Galway, Ireland, the company built its product philosophy on Getting Things Done (GTD) methodology applied to sales pipelines.

Rather than a traditional dashboard-heavy CRM, OnePageCRM presents an action stream where sales reps see exactly what to do next for each lead or deal, reducing the cognitive overhead common in larger, more complex CRM platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core Next Action system assigns every contact a single follow-up task, which surfaces in a prioritized queue similar to an email inbox. Alongside this, OnePageCRM offers a visual sales pipeline, email templates and integration, and web forms for capturing leads directly into the CRM.

Additional capabilities include workflow automation rules, basic lead scoring, customizable tags and fields, and team collaboration tools like shared notes and shared communication history, all wrapped in a deliberately lightweight, low-clutter interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OnePageCRM has no permanently free tier but offers a 21-day free trial. The Professional plan starts at $9.95 per user per month on an annual billing cycle, covering the core Next Action CRM, pipeline, and contact management features.
The Business plan is $19.95 per user per month and adds more advanced automation and reporting for growing teams. Annual billing includes several months free compared to month-to-month pricing.

features.title: Next Action selling system
features.description: Every contact is assigned a single, concrete next action that surfaces in a prioritized action stream, keeping sales reps focused on follow-up.

features.title: Visual sales pipeline
features.description: A drag-and-drop pipeline view for tracking deals through custom stages alongside the Next Action queue.

features.title: Email integration and templates
features.description: Send and track emails from within the CRM using reusable templates and two-way email sync.

features.title: Web forms for lead capture
features.description: Embeddable web forms feed new leads directly into the CRM with automatic contact creation.

features.title: Workflow automation
features.description: Automation rules trigger follow-up tasks and status changes based on contact activity.

features.title: Customizable tags and fields
features.description: Flexible tagging and custom fields let small teams adapt the CRM to their specific sales process.

features.title: Team collaboration
features.description: Shared notes and communication history keep small sales teams aligned on account context.

pros: Next Action system provides clear daily focus, reducing decision paralysis common in larger CRMs
Fast onboarding and minimal learning curve compared to enterprise CRM platforms
Lightweight, uncluttered interface with no unnecessary bloat
Affordable entry pricing well suited to small businesses and solo founders
Responsive, small-team customer support
cons: No permanently free plan, only a 21-day trial
Fewer advanced reporting and customization options than larger CRMs like Salesforce or HubSpot
Smaller third-party integration ecosystem than major CRM competitors
Business plan pricing at $19.95/user/month may be steep for very small teams needing advanced features
Mobile app functionality is more limited than the desktop web experience

faqs.question: What is the Next Action system in OnePageCRM?
faqs.answer: The Next Action system assigns every contact a single, concrete follow-up task, which appears in a prioritized action stream. It is based on Getting Things Done (GTD) methodology applied to sales, designed to keep reps focused on what to do next rather than passively browsing a dashboard.

faqs.question: How much does OnePageCRM cost?
faqs.answer: OnePageCRM's Professional plan starts at $9.95 per user per month (annual billing), and the Business plan is $19.95 per user per month. There is no permanent free plan, but a 21-day free trial is available.

faqs.question: Who is OnePageCRM best suited for?
faqs.answer: OnePageCRM is designed for small and medium-sized businesses, solo founders, freelancers, and small sales teams who find larger CRMs like Salesforce too complex or expensive.

faqs.question: Where is OnePageCRM based?
faqs.answer: OnePageCRM was founded in 2010 and is headquartered in Galway, Ireland, with a small team of roughly 20-25 employees.

faqs.question: Does OnePageCRM integrate with email?
faqs.answer: Yes, OnePageCRM offers email integration and templates, allowing users to send and track emails directly from within the CRM.

faqs.question: How does OnePageCRM compare to Pipedrive?
faqs.answer: Both target small businesses with simple, focused CRMs. OnePageCRM emphasizes its action-oriented Next Action queue, while Pipedrive centers more on a visual deal pipeline, though the two overlap significantly in target audience and pricing tier.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for OnePageCRM?
faqs.answer: Yes, OnePageCRM offers a 21-day free trial on its paid plans, with no permanent free tier.

use_cases.title: Solo founder sales tracking
use_cases.description: Solo founders and freelancers use the Next Action queue to stay on top of every lead without needing a full sales operations setup.

use_cases.title: Small B2B sales team pipeline management
use_cases.description: Small sales teams track deals through a visual pipeline while ensuring every contact has a clear follow-up action assigned.

use_cases.title: Consulting and professional services client follow-up
use_cases.description: Consulting firms use OnePageCRM to manage client relationships and ensure timely follow-up on proposals and engagements.

enrichment.short_description: OneSignal is a customer engagement platform for sending push notifications, email, SMS, and in-app messages, powering over 8 billion messages a day for mobile and web apps.
enrichment.starting_price: Free; Growth plan from $19/month plus usage
enrichment.seo_title: OneSignal Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OneSignal review 2026: push notifications, email, SMS and in-app messaging for apps. Compare pricing plans, features and top alternatives like Braze.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OneSignal is a customer engagement and messaging platform founded in 2011 by George Deglin and Long Vo, headquartered in San Mateo, California, and backed by roughly $84.2 million in total funding from investors including SignalFire, Y Combinator, and HubSpot. The platform unifies push notifications (mobile and web), email, SMS, and in-app messaging into a single dashboard, and by its own account delivers more than 8 billion messages per day, making it one of the most widely adopted messaging infrastructure providers for mobile and web developers. OneSignal's key differentiator versus larger customer engagement suites like Braze or Iterable is its free, generous entry tier combined with developer-friendly SDKs across iOS, Android, web, Unity, React Native, Flutter, and Cordova, which made it a default choice for indie developers and startups needing reliable push notifications without a steep initial cost. The platform includes audience segmentation, automated messaging journeys with multi-step flows, A/B testing, GDPR-compliant data handling, delivery analytics, and an 'Intelligent Delivery' feature that optimizes send timing per user. OneSignal's pricing follows a freemium, usage-based model: the Free plan includes 10,000 monthly email sends and unlimited mobile push notifications, with a single active messaging journey; the Growth plan starts at $19/month plus usage-based costs (email at $1.50 per 1,000 sends beyond the included allotment, mobile push at $0.012 per monthly active user, web push at $0.004 per subscriber) and unlocks unlimited in-app messaging and more journeys and segments; the annually billed Professional tier offers custom, volume-based pricing with expanded journeys, advanced A/B testing, conversion tracking, and a dedicated IP option; and the Enterprise tier offers fully custom contracts with unlimited journeys, frequency capping, a dedicated success manager, extended audit logs, and SLA guarantees. OneSignal targets mobile and web app developers, product teams, and growth/marketing teams at companies ranging from indie app studios to large consumer apps that need reliable, high-volume, multi-channel messaging infrastructure. Its primary competitors include Braze, Airship, CleverTap, Iterable, and Firebase Cloud Messaging (Google's free but more limited push notification service), with OneSignal generally positioned as the more developer-accessible, cost-effective option compared to enterprise-focused platforms like Braze.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OneSignal is a customer engagement platform that unifies push notifications, email, SMS, and in-app messaging for mobile and web applications. Founded in 2011 in San Mateo, California, it has grown into one of the most widely used messaging infrastructure providers, delivering more than 8 billion messages per day.

The platform is particularly popular among app developers because of its generous free tier and straightforward SDK integration across major mobile and web platforms, making it a common default choice for startups and indie developers before they scale into larger engagement platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OneSignal supports multi-channel messaging (push, email, SMS, in-app) from a single dashboard, with audience segmentation, automated multi-step messaging journeys, and A/B testing built in.

Its 'Intelligent Delivery' feature optimizes send timing per individual user, and the platform provides delivery analytics, conversion tracking, and GDPR-compliant data handling for teams operating in regulated markets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OneSignal's Free plan covers 10,000 monthly email sends and unlimited mobile push notifications, making it usable at no cost for small apps.

The Growth plan starts at $19/month plus usage-based fees, while Professional and Enterprise tiers offer custom, volume-based pricing with expanded journeys, segments, and support for larger organizations.

features.title: Multi-channel messaging
features.description: Send push notifications, email, SMS, and in-app messages from a single unified dashboard and API.

features.title: Automated messaging journeys
features.description: Build multi-step, triggered messaging flows based on user behavior and lifecycle stage.

features.title: Audience segmentation
features.description: Segment users by behavior, custom data tags, and engagement history for targeted campaigns.

features.title: Intelligent Delivery
features.description: Automatically optimizes send timing for each user to maximize open and engagement rates.

features.title: A/B testing
features.description: Test message variants across channels to optimize copy, timing, and creative.

features.title: Cross-platform SDKs
features.description: Native SDKs for iOS, Android, web, Unity, React Native, Flutter, and Cordova simplify integration.

features.title: Delivery and conversion analytics
features.description: Track delivery rates, opens, clicks, and conversions across all messaging channels.

pros: Generous free tier with unlimited mobile push notifications makes it accessible for small apps
Delivers billions of messages daily, demonstrating proven scale and reliability
Broad SDK support across iOS, Android, web, Unity, React Native, Flutter, and Cordova
Unified multi-channel approach avoids stitching together separate push, email, and SMS tools
Backed by significant venture funding ($84.2M), supporting continued product investment
cons: Usage-based pricing on the Growth plan can become costly as email and push volume scales
Professional and Enterprise pricing is custom and not transparently published
Advanced personalization and journey features are gated behind higher tiers
Smaller brand recognition in the enterprise segment compared to Braze or Iterable
Free plan's single active journey limit is restrictive for more complex campaigns

faqs.question: Is OneSignal free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, OneSignal offers a free plan that includes 10,000 monthly email sends and unlimited mobile push notification sends, along with basic segmentation and A/B testing, suitable for small apps and early-stage products.

faqs.question: How much does OneSignal cost beyond the free plan?
faqs.answer: The Growth plan starts at $19/month plus usage-based costs (for example, $1.50 per 1,000 emails beyond the included allotment, and $0.012 per monthly active user for mobile push). Professional and Enterprise tiers use custom, volume-based pricing.

faqs.question: What channels does OneSignal support?
faqs.answer: OneSignal supports mobile push notifications, web push notifications, email, SMS, and in-app messaging, all managed from a single dashboard.

faqs.question: Who founded OneSignal and when?
faqs.answer: OneSignal was founded in 2011 by George Deglin and Long Vo, and is headquartered in San Mateo, California.

faqs.question: How does OneSignal compare to Firebase Cloud Messaging?
faqs.answer: Firebase Cloud Messaging is a free, more basic push notification service from Google, while OneSignal offers a more full-featured platform with segmentation, automated journeys, multi-channel messaging, and analytics, available on both free and paid tiers.

faqs.question: Does OneSignal support automated messaging journeys?
faqs.answer: Yes, OneSignal supports multi-step automated messaging journeys, with the number of active journeys and steps expanding across higher-priced plans.

faqs.question: How much venture funding has OneSignal raised?
faqs.answer: OneSignal has raised approximately $84.2 million in total funding across multiple rounds, from investors including SignalFire, Y Combinator, and HubSpot.

enrichment.short_description: Onloc is a free, open-source, self-hosted device tracking platform that lets users locate, monitor, and remotely control their own Android devices without relying on Google's Find My Device service.
enrichment.seo_title: Onloc Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Onloc review 2026: a free, self-hosted, open-source device tracking tool for de-Googled Android phones. See features, setup, and alternatives like OwnTracks.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Onloc is a free, open-source, self-hosted device tracking and control platform designed primarily for de-Googled Android devices, giving privacy-conscious users a way to locate, monitor, and remotely control their own phones and devices without depending on Google's Find My Device or similar proprietary cloud services. The project is maintained by a community of open-source contributors (credited on the project as 'Onloc Maintainers' rather than a commercial company) and is distributed across several GitHub repositories: the main onloc deployment repository, an onloc-api backend that manages location data, an onloc-android client app, and a web frontend for managing devices and viewing location history. Core functionality includes real-time and historical location tracking of enrolled devices, the ability to send remote commands such as ring and lock to a lost or stolen device, and sharing device location with trusted friends or family members. Because it is self-hosted, all location data stays on infrastructure the user controls rather than being sent to a third-party cloud provider, which is the platform's central value proposition for privacy-focused users, including those running de-Googled Android distributions like GrapheneOS, LineageOS, or CalyxOS where Google's own Find My Device is unavailable. The Android client can be installed through F-Droid, direct GitHub releases, or Obtainium, all common distribution channels for privacy-respecting open-source Android apps. Onloc also offers a Home Assistant integration, letting users incorporate device location into broader smart-home automations. Onloc is entirely free with no paid tiers, monetization, or company behind it; users self-host the API and frontend (for example via Docker) on their own server or a small VPS, and there is no subscription or licensing cost. Its target audience is privacy-focused individuals, self-hosting enthusiasts, and de-Googled Android users who want Find My Device-style functionality without sending location data to Google or any third party. Its closest competitors and alternatives include OwnTracks (a longer-established self-hosted location-sharing tool), Traccar (a more general-purpose open-source GPS tracking platform), Nextcloud's location-sharing capabilities, and GPSLogger, with Onloc differentiating itself through its specific focus on Find My Device-style remote device commands (ring, lock) rather than pure location logging.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Onloc is a free, open-source, self-hosted alternative to Google's Find My Device, built specifically with de-Googled Android users in mind. It lets device owners track location history, share location with trusted contacts, and send remote commands like ring and lock to their own devices.

The project is community-maintained rather than run by a commercial company, and is split across several open-source repositories covering the backend API, Android client, and web frontend, all of which users deploy on their own server infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Onloc's core feature is real-time and historical device location tracking, viewable through a self-hosted web frontend. Device owners can also send remote commands, including ring and lock, to help recover a lost or stolen device.

The Android client is distributed through F-Droid, GitHub releases, and Obtainium, and the platform includes a Home Assistant integration for incorporating device location into broader home automation setups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Onloc is entirely free and open source, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or licensing fees. Users are responsible only for the cost of hosting their own server, such as a small VPS or home server running Docker.
Because there is no commercial entity behind Onloc, there are no enterprise or support-tier pricing options; support comes from the open-source community via GitHub.

features.title: Real-time location tracking
features.description: View the current location and location history of enrolled Android devices from a self-hosted dashboard.

features.title: Remote device commands
features.description: Send ring and lock commands remotely to help locate or secure a lost or stolen device.

features.title: Location sharing
features.description: Share a device's location with trusted friends or family members.

features.title: De-Googled device support
features.description: Built specifically to work on Android distributions without Google Play Services, such as GrapheneOS and LineageOS.

features.title: Home Assistant integration
features.description: Incorporate device location data into Home Assistant automations and dashboards.

features.title: Self-hosted data ownership
features.description: All location data stays on infrastructure the user controls, with no third-party cloud dependency.

features.title: Multiple installation channels
features.description: Android client available via F-Droid, direct GitHub releases, and Obtainium.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers or hidden costs
Full data ownership since all location data stays on self-hosted infrastructure
Purpose-built for de-Googled Android devices that lack access to Google's Find My Device
Includes Find My Device-style remote commands like ring and lock, not just passive tracking
Home Assistant integration extends functionality into broader smart-home setups
cons: Requires technical ability to self-host, unlike consumer-friendly commercial alternatives
No official mobile app for iOS, limiting it to Android devices
Community-maintained project with no formal support SLA or dedicated company backing
Smaller feature set than mature commercial fleet-tracking or family-safety apps
Relies on the user maintaining their own server uptime and security patching

faqs.question: Is Onloc free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Onloc is completely free and open source. There are no paid tiers or subscription fees; users only pay for their own server hosting if they choose to use a VPS rather than a home server.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host Onloc?
faqs.answer: Yes, Onloc is a self-hosted platform. Users deploy the API and web frontend on their own server, commonly via Docker, and there is no official managed cloud version.

faqs.question: Does Onloc work without Google Play Services?
faqs.answer: Yes, Onloc was built specifically to support de-Googled Android devices, including distributions like GrapheneOS, LineageOS, and CalyxOS, where Google's Find My Device is unavailable.

faqs.question: What remote commands can Onloc send to a device?
faqs.answer: Onloc supports remote commands including ring (to help locate a nearby device) and lock (to help secure a lost or stolen device).

faqs.question: How do I install the Onloc Android app?
faqs.answer: The Onloc Android client can be installed via F-Droid, downloaded directly from GitHub releases, or installed and auto-updated through Obtainium.

faqs.question: Does Onloc support iOS devices?
faqs.answer: No, Onloc's client app is currently Android-only, targeting de-Googled and privacy-focused Android users specifically.

faqs.question: Who maintains Onloc?
faqs.answer: Onloc is maintained by a community of open-source contributors credited as 'Onloc Maintainers,' rather than a commercial company, with development happening in the open on GitHub.

use_cases.title: De-Googled phone tracking
use_cases.description: Users running GrapheneOS, LineageOS, or similar de-Googled Android distributions use Onloc to regain Find My Device-style tracking without Google services.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted family device sharing
use_cases.description: Families share device locations with each other through a self-hosted Onloc instance instead of a third-party cloud service.

use_cases.title: Smart-home location automation
use_cases.description: Home Assistant users integrate Onloc device location data into automations, such as adjusting climate or lighting based on arrival.

enrichment.short_description: ONLYOFFICE is an open-source-based office suite and collaboration workspace offering online and desktop editors for documents, spreadsheets, presentations, forms, and PDFs, available cloud-hosted or self-hosted.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Community edition and personal cloud); Enterprise from $1,500/year
enrichment.seo_title: ONLYOFFICE Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ONLYOFFICE review 2026: open-source office suite with docs, spreadsheets, and presentations. Compare cloud vs self-hosted pricing and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ONLYOFFICE is an office productivity suite developed by Ascensio System SIA, a software company founded in 2009 and headquartered in Riga, Latvia, with additional offices in Singapore, London, Dallas, Belgrade, Yerevan, Tashkent, and Shanghai, and an internationally distributed team of open-source contributors from more than 30 countries. Led by founder and CEO Lev Bannov, the company built ONLYOFFICE as an open-source-core alternative to Microsoft Office and Google Workspace, offering online and desktop editors for text documents, spreadsheets, presentations, fillable forms, and PDFs with high compatibility with Microsoft Office file formats (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx). ONLYOFFICE's key differentiator is deployment flexibility: unlike Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace, which are cloud-only, ONLYOFFICE can be fully self-hosted on-premises for organizations with data sovereignty, compliance, or air-gapped requirements, while also offering managed cloud hosting for teams that want a SaaS experience. The product line includes ONLYOFFICE Docs (the core document editors, available as an open-source Community edition and a licensed Enterprise edition), ONLYOFFICE Workspace (a fuller collaboration suite adding email, CRM, project management, and document management alongside the editors), and ONLYOFFICE DocSpace (a room-based document collaboration and sharing product). ONLYOFFICE Docs Enterprise pricing starts at $1,500 per year for a license covering 50 user connections, deployable either in the cloud or on-premises, with a choice between a 1-year license (requiring annual renewal) or a lifetime license that includes three years of updates; buyers can also select support tiers (Basic, Plus, Premium) offering faster response times, and add-ons like a live document viewer, training courses, disaster recovery, and multi-server deployment support. A free Community edition and a free personal cloud plan are also available for individuals and small teams with lighter usage needs. ONLYOFFICE targets a broad range of customers, from individual users and small businesses wanting a free Microsoft Office alternative, to enterprises, government agencies, and regulated industries (finance, healthcare, legal) that specifically need self-hosted, on-premises document editing for compliance reasons. Its primary competitors are Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, LibreOffice, Collabora Online (another self-hostable open-source suite), and Zoho Office Suite, with ONLYOFFICE differentiating itself through the combination of strong Microsoft Office format compatibility, an open-source Community edition, and flexible on-premises deployment that few competitors offer at comparable price points.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ONLYOFFICE is an office productivity suite developed by Ascensio System SIA, founded in 2009 and headquartered in Riga, Latvia. It provides online and desktop editors for documents, spreadsheets, presentations, forms, and PDFs, with strong compatibility for Microsoft Office file formats.

What sets ONLYOFFICE apart from Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace is deployment flexibility: it is available as a free, open-source Community edition that organizations can self-host on-premises, in addition to cloud-hosted and licensed Enterprise options.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ONLYOFFICE Docs offers full-featured document, spreadsheet, presentation, form, and PDF editors, accessible via web browser, desktop app, or mobile app, with real-time co-editing and commenting for team collaboration.

ONLYOFFICE Workspace expands the offering with email, CRM, project management, and document management modules, while ONLYOFFICE DocSpace focuses on room-based document sharing and external collaboration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ONLYOFFICE Docs Enterprise starts at $1,500 per year for a 50-user license, available as cloud-hosted or on-premises, with a choice of a renewable 1-year license or a lifetime license including three years of updates.

A free, open-source Community edition and a free personal cloud plan are also available for individuals and small teams, with paid support tiers and add-ons like disaster recovery and training available for Enterprise customers.

features.title: Full office editor suite
features.description: Online and desktop editors for text documents, spreadsheets, presentations, and PDFs with strong Microsoft Office format compatibility.

features.title: Fillable forms editor
features.description: Create and fill out interactive PDF and OFORM forms directly within the suite.

features.title: Real-time co-editing
features.description: Multiple users can edit documents simultaneously with live cursors, comments, and version tracking.

features.title: On-premises deployment
features.description: Fully self-hostable on an organization's own infrastructure for data sovereignty and compliance needs.

features.title: Open-source Community edition
features.description: A free, open-source edition of ONLYOFFICE Docs available for individuals and small teams.

features.title: Workspace collaboration suite
features.description: Optional bundled email, CRM, project management, and document management modules beyond the core editors.

features.title: Third-party integrations
features.description: Ready-to-use connectors for platforms like Nextcloud, Confluence, ownCloud, and various DMS systems.

pros: Available fully self-hosted on-premises, unlike cloud-only competitors like Google Workspace
Free, open-source Community edition provides genuine no-cost access to core editing features
Strong compatibility with Microsoft Office file formats reduces migration friction
Flexible licensing (1-year or lifetime) gives organizations control over long-term costs
Global support office footprint across Europe, Asia, and North America
cons: Enterprise pricing starting at $1,500/year for 50 users can be a significant upfront cost for small teams
Real-time collaboration and polish can lag slightly behind Google Docs or Microsoft 365 in some edge cases
Self-hosting requires ongoing server maintenance and IT resources
Support response times vary significantly by tier (Basic support has a 48-hour response window)
Smaller ecosystem of third-party templates and add-ons compared to Microsoft or Google

faqs.question: Is ONLYOFFICE free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, ONLYOFFICE offers a free, open-source Community edition of its Docs editors and a free personal cloud plan. The Enterprise edition, which starts at $1,500/year for 50 users, adds enhanced security, support, and deployment options.

faqs.question: Can ONLYOFFICE be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, ONLYOFFICE can be fully self-hosted on-premises, which is one of its main differentiators from cloud-only suites like Google Workspace and standard Microsoft 365.

faqs.question: Is ONLYOFFICE compatible with Microsoft Office files?
faqs.answer: Yes, ONLYOFFICE offers strong compatibility with Microsoft Office formats including .docx, .xlsx, and .pptx, making it easier for organizations to migrate from Microsoft Office.

faqs.question: Who develops ONLYOFFICE?
faqs.answer: ONLYOFFICE is developed by Ascensio System SIA, a company founded in 2009 and headquartered in Riga, Latvia, led by founder and CEO Lev Bannov.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ONLYOFFICE Docs and Workspace?
faqs.answer: ONLYOFFICE Docs is the core document, spreadsheet, and presentation editor suite. ONLYOFFICE Workspace is a broader collaboration platform that adds email, CRM, project management, and document management on top of the Docs editors.

faqs.question: How much does ONLYOFFICE Enterprise cost?
faqs.answer: ONLYOFFICE Docs Enterprise starts at $1,500 per year for a license covering 50 user connections, with options for cloud or on-premises deployment and a choice between a renewable 1-year license or a lifetime license.

faqs.question: What are ONLYOFFICE's main alternatives?
faqs.answer: Its main competitors include Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, LibreOffice, Collabora Online, and Zoho Office Suite.

use_cases.title: Compliance-driven document editing

use_cases.title: Cost-conscious Microsoft Office replacement

enrichment.short_description: Onyx Community Edition is the free, MIT-licensed, open-source version of Onyx (formerly Danswer), an AI enterprise search and assistant platform that connects to 40+ knowledge sources for chat, RAG, and autonomous agents.
enrichment.seo_title: Onyx Community Edition Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Onyx Community Edition review 2026: free, open-source AI enterprise search (formerly Danswer). Compare features, self-hosting, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Onyx is an open-source AI platform for enterprise search, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and AI agents, founded in 2023 by Yuhong Sun and Chris Weaver and headquartered in San Francisco. The company was originally known as Danswer before rebranding to Onyx, and it raised a $10 million seed round co-led by Khosla Ventures and First Round Capital, with participation from Y Combinator and angel investors including Gokul Rajaram, Arash Ferdosi, and Amit Agarwal; it employs roughly 20 people as of its most recent public profile. Onyx Community Edition (CE) is the fully free, MIT-licensed, self-hostable version of the platform, and unlike many 'open core' products, it is not a stripped-down trial: Onyx states that CE includes the complete core feature set for Chat, RAG, Agents, and Actions, including connectors to more than 40 knowledge sources such as Slack, Google Drive, Confluence, Salesforce, and GitHub, letting organizations build an internal, ChatGPT-style assistant that can answer questions grounded in their own private data while working with any LLM provider of their choice (OpenAI, Anthropic, self-hosted open-weight models, etc.). Beyond basic chat and search, Onyx supports building custom AI agents, deep research workflows across connected knowledge sources, and integration with the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for tool use. For organizations that do not want to manage self-hosted infrastructure, Onyx also offers a managed Cloud edition starting around $16-20 per seat per month (with a higher monthly, lower annual rate), and an Enterprise Edition (EE) — available both self-hosted and in the cloud — that adds enterprise governance features including SOC 2 Type II compliance, SSO via OIDC and SAML, SCIM/identity provider integration, granular role-based access control, and audit trails, priced via custom contracts. Onyx's target audience is engineering-savvy organizations, IT and data teams, and enterprises with strict data-residency or compliance requirements that need an AI search and assistant layer over internal knowledge without sending proprietary data to a third-party SaaS vendor, as well as smaller technical teams that want a capable, free, self-hosted AI knowledge assistant. Its primary competitors include Glean (a well-funded enterprise search leader), Guru, Elastic Enterprise Search, Microsoft Copilot for enterprise search scenarios, and Perplexity Enterprise, with Onyx differentiating itself through its genuinely open-source, MIT-licensed core and the option to self-host with full control over both data and choice of underlying LLM.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Onyx is an open-source AI platform for enterprise search, retrieval-augmented generation, and AI agents, founded in 2023 by Yuhong Sun and Chris Weaver in San Francisco. The project was originally launched under the name Danswer before rebranding to Onyx.

Onyx Community Edition is the free, MIT-licensed, self-hostable version of the platform, offering the complete core feature set rather than a limited trial, letting technical teams build an internal AI assistant grounded in their own company data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Onyx connects to more than 40 knowledge sources, including Slack, Google Drive, Confluence, Salesforce, and GitHub, indexing internal content so users can ask natural-language questions and get answers grounded in that data via retrieval-augmented generation.

The platform supports custom AI agents, deep research workflows across connected sources, Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration for tool use, and works with any LLM provider, letting organizations choose between commercial APIs like OpenAI and Anthropic or self-hosted open-weight models.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Onyx Community Edition is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no artificial feature limits on core Chat, RAG, Agents, and Actions capabilities for self-hosted deployments.

Onyx also offers a managed Cloud plan starting around $16-20 per seat per month for teams that prefer not to manage infrastructure, plus a custom-priced Enterprise Edition adding SOC 2 compliance, SSO, SCIM, and audit trails for larger organizations.

features.title: AI chat grounded in company data
features.description: A ChatGPT-style assistant that answers questions using retrieval-augmented generation over indexed internal knowledge sources.

features.title: 40+ knowledge source connectors
features.description: Native connectors to Slack, Google Drive, Confluence, Salesforce, GitHub, and dozens of other enterprise tools.

features.title: Custom AI agents
features.description: Build task-specific AI agents that can take actions across connected systems, not just answer questions.

features.title: LLM provider flexibility
features.description: Works with any LLM provider, including OpenAI, Anthropic, and self-hosted open-weight models.

features.title: Deep research workflows
features.description: Multi-step research capabilities that synthesize answers across many connected knowledge sources.

features.title: MCP tool integration
features.description: Supports the Model Context Protocol for connecting AI agents to external tools and actions.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Deployable via Docker, Kubernetes, or Helm/Terraform for full data control and on-premises compliance.

pros: Community Edition is genuinely free and MIT-licensed with the full core feature set, not a crippled trial
Works with any LLM provider, avoiding vendor lock-in to a single AI model
Broad library of 40+ pre-built connectors to common enterprise knowledge sources
Backed by significant venture funding ($10M seed from Khosla Ventures and First Round Capital), supporting active development
Self-hosting option gives full data control for compliance-sensitive organizations
cons: Self-hosting requires technical infrastructure expertise (Docker/Kubernetes) to deploy and maintain
Enterprise governance features like SSO, SCIM, and audit trails require the paid Enterprise Edition
Managed Cloud pricing (from $16-20/seat/month) is per-seat, which can scale up quickly for larger teams
Younger company (founded 2023) with a smaller support and services organization than established competitors like Glean
Rebrand from Danswer to Onyx may cause confusion when researching historical reviews or documentation

faqs.question: Is Onyx the same as Danswer?
faqs.answer: Yes. Onyx was originally launched under the name Danswer before the company rebranded to Onyx. The underlying open-source project and GitHub organization now use the Onyx name.

faqs.question: Is Onyx Community Edition really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Onyx Community Edition is free and open source under the MIT license, and includes the full core feature set for Chat, RAG, Agents, and Actions when self-hosted.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Onyx Community and Enterprise Edition?
faqs.answer: Community Edition covers the core AI chat, search, and agent features for free. Enterprise Edition adds governance and compliance features like SOC 2 Type II, SSO via OIDC/SAML, SCIM identity provider integration, granular RBAC, and audit trails, available via custom pricing.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Onyx?
faqs.answer: Yes, Onyx supports self-hosted deployment via Docker, Kubernetes, or Helm/Terraform, giving organizations full control over their data and infrastructure.

faqs.question: How much does Onyx Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Onyx's managed Cloud plan starts at approximately $16-20 per seat per month depending on billing terms, with Enterprise pricing available on request for larger deployments.

faqs.question: Who founded Onyx?
faqs.answer: Onyx was founded in 2023 by Yuhong Sun and Chris Weaver, and is headquartered in San Francisco. The company raised a $10 million seed round co-led by Khosla Ventures and First Round Capital.

faqs.question: Which LLM providers does Onyx support?
faqs.answer: Onyx is LLM-agnostic and works with commercial providers like OpenAI and Anthropic as well as self-hosted open-weight models, letting organizations choose based on cost, performance, or compliance needs.

use_cases.title: Internal enterprise knowledge search
use_cases.description: Organizations index Slack, Confluence, Google Drive, and other internal tools so employees can ask natural-language questions and get grounded answers.

use_cases.title: Compliance-sensitive AI deployment
use_cases.description: Regulated organizations self-host Onyx Community or Enterprise Edition to keep proprietary data and AI processing entirely within their own infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Custom AI agent development
use_cases.description: Technical teams build custom AI agents on top of Onyx that can search company data and take actions across connected systems via MCP.

features: Full Chat, RAG, Agents, and Actions feature set
